sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting level 1. The board shows a blue background with several colored objects. Need to understand the layout and goal.
- Plan: Inspect the segmentation to identify all objects, their colors, positions, and relationships.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see that the dark gray shape has moved! It was at [[51, 27], [51, 29], [56, 29], [56, 27]] and now it's at [[48, 33], [48, 38], [50, 38], [50, 33]]. The dark gray shape still has 18 pixels. The yellow strip went from 18 to 16 pixels.
Looking at the image, the character has moved up and the dark gray shape is now to the right of the character. The character is now at the divider area.
Wait, I think I understand now. The dark gray shape is NOT being "eaten" by the character. Instead, the dark gray shape moves when the character crosses the divider. The yellow strip on the right edge is shrinking as a timer/progress indicator.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics:
1. The character starts on the left side
2. The dark gray L-shape starts on the right side
3. When the character crosses the divider to the right, the dark gray shape moves to the left
4. The yellow L-shape at bottom right is the target
5. The goal might be to position the character so that the dark gray shape ends up in the same position as the yellow L-shape
But the dark gray shape has shrunk from 45 to 18 pixels. This suggests that parts of it were "eaten" or removed.
Actually, looking more carefully at the boundaries:
- Initial dark gray: [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] with 45 pixels - this is an L-shape
- Current dark gray: [[48, 33], [48, 38], [50, 38], [50, 33]] with 18 pixels - this is a 3x5 rectangle
The dark gray shape has changed from an L-shape to a rectangle. This is strange.
Wait, maybe the dark gray shape is being "clipped" by the character. When the character overlaps with the dark gray shape, the overlapping parts are removed. The character is 8x8 (40 pixels), and the dark gray L-shape is larger. When they overlap, the overlapping pixels are removed from the dark gray shape.
Let me think about this differently. The initial dark gray L-shape has 45 pixels. After the character crossed the divider and overlapped with it, it reduced to 18 pixels. The character is an 8x8 L-shape (40 pixels). The overlap removed 27 pixels from the dark gray shape.
Now the dark gray shape is a 3x5 rectangle (18 pixels) at [[48, 33], [48, 38], [50, 38], [50, 33]]. The character is at [[48, 24], [48, 32], [56, 32], [56, 30], [51, 30], [50, 29], [50, 24]].
The dark gray shape is to the right of the character. To "eat" it, I need to move the character to overlap with it. Let me move RIGHT to overlap with the dark gray shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
char = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 40][0]
dark_gray = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'c'][0]

print(f"Char: boundary={char['boundary']}")
print(f"Dark gray: boundary={dark_gray['boundary']}, pixels={dark_gray['pixels']}")

# Dark gray is at rows 48-50, cols 33-38 (3x5 rectangle)
# Char is at rows 48-56, cols 24-32
# Need to move RIGHT to overlap with dark gray
# Char right edge is at col 32, dark gray left edge is at col 33
# Move RIGHT 1 to start overlapping

action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
